speaker: interviewer
text: Number 8-- an interview with Emanuel Mittelman. OK, the last question I have for you-- not to mention Majdanek or the death marches or anything, but the life expectancy at Auschwitz was 10 weeks, and you survived three years. I mean, looking back over all of your experiences, what do you attribute your survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] God's hand. I've repeated this many times to many people. We in America are used to the game of baseball. And we have batters going to bat. And you evaluate them by the guys batting 300 and 250 and 400 and 500 and so forth. 500's a tremendous-- you probably can't find one who bats 500. That means every time you go-- every two times you go to bat, you hit once. I lost my parents and my two brothers and my sister to one shooting in Poland, as I mentioned to you before. Here are three brothers and my one sister who endured the whole three years of concentration camps, and everyone came back. That is batting 1,000. And nobody in this world who listens to this tape will be able to say because I had a hard life maybe as a child that I was able to endure this because it doesn't make any sense if you exactly heard what I've been through. It wasn't because we were stronger built at home. It wasn't because it was something in our genes. Nothing of that will make sense. I personally believe if there is such a thing as destiny, and it's willed by God. Otherwise, it's beyond comprehension how somebody can bat 1,000 in three years concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you for sharing your testimony. We're going to pause here.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're viewing a picture that was made in 1930 in Michalovce. Going from the bottom up, on the right-hand side is my brother Joe. I am in the middle, five years old. On the left-hand side on the bottom is my brother, who never came back from concentration camp. Above his head is my sister, who never came back from concentration camp. And then you see my parents. And above that, you see on the left-hand side, my aunt, on the right-hand side, my cousin. And in the center is a tenant who rented one of the rooms in our house who was helpful in making that picture. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The picture you're viewing now is our marriage ceremony in Zurich, Switzerland, in the year of 1949. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You're viewing a picture of my employment in Zurich, Switzerland, in 1950, where I was working as a kosher milkman for the congregation, [NON-ENGLISH]. And was just right after I married. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You're viewing a picture in 1954 in May where we were photographed by a large magazine in Switzerland as we leave the country to go to the United States. And our first three children, who are born in Switzerland, of course, are pictured there. The little one is not seen. But our good friends with whom we are still friends up until today. Mrs. [? Warmser ?] is seen in that picture, too. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You're viewing a picture of my daughter Naomi's wedding with my wife and myself being on the side of her and my-- a few of my grandchildren, six of them, in the front of us. Then my son-in-law Paul Cohn and his wife Leah are the top left. And my son David is next. And my daughter Este is next, and my son-in-law, Dr. Schwartz. In the next one, my daughter Dena, and then my son with his youngest daughter shown in the picture. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mannie, I see your family has joined you today. Would you like to please introduce them for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, correct is really that part of my family has joined me. And I'll be more than happy to introduce them if I still remember all their names. So let's start first with my children. To my left is my daughter Dena. And she spells her name, D-E-N-A. She always emphasis on that. That's her little baby. She likes to call her Luella. My other daughter--

speaker: interviewee
text: What's her real name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schwartz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rebecca.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rebecca Schwartz. My youngest daughter, Naomi-- she's a Rottenstein. She's married to my nephew from Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll find him later. That's my daughter Lomascha. She's married to David. And I don't know who's the next one. And this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Michally. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Michally ?] Rottenstein, Naomi's [? Yankel's ?] daughter. And this one here with a baseball cap who's hiding his face is a Schwartz. What's your first name? Joshua Schwartz. [LAUGHTER] Behind him, the tall one is the basketball player, Gabriel. And next to him, is my lovey dovey, Devorah Cohn. And in front of her here-- see if I see correct-- is Sarah Eliza Rottenstein. And next to her, to her right is Sarah Eliza Cohn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandaughter. This one-- my grandaughter. Of course they know it's my grandaughter. This is my grandson, Denia Yisrael Aye, who's named after my brother who never came back. That's my grandson Elie who has already a daughter on his own who is not here. This is my son-in-law Yankel Rottenstein from Israel. And this is my lovely son, the doctor, PhD, David Mittelman. And my oldest daughter, Leah Cohn, who's married to Paul who's not here either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's see now. This is Jano. He's the youngest-- not the youngest, but youngest son of my daughter, Dena Schwartz. And this is my bestest with 10,000 S's. What's your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Avi. Avi Cohn. And this one is my best Hebrew reader, and that is Bella Mittelman. And as you see, that's only a part of my family because some of them are in New York and they couldn't make it here. But I'm thankful to the almighty that I have them, at least, around me, and I'm very appreciative about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, Nana. OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nana.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead, Leah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Speak up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's very obvious that we're all immensely proud of my dad, and he has tremendous spirit. The Nazis and the Holocaust tried to break the human beings physically and emotionally, and they tried to break their spirit. But there's no one in the world that could break my father's spirit. [BABY CRYING] Even after everything he went through, he never went through life expecting that the world owed him something. He was thankful every day that God had given him a second chance. And he's a remarkable man with tremendous strength and fortitude that could teach us lessons every hour of every day. And I'm grateful that all of us have him as a shining and joyous example of how to live and the way to live. And I'm personally thrilled, and I know all my brothers and sisters are, that we had such a special man to raise us and to be able to be a part of our lives and our children's lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Leah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'd like to add, Dad, that probably all of us could imagine that for as many reasons as you would have for wanting to leave on film this kind of living testimony to some of your earlier experiences in your life, you probably have just as many reasons for why, maybe, you might not have wanted to leave such a testimony. And especially in view of that and the difficulty we're sure that it posed, just, you know, having to think about some of the difficulties that you experienced in the Holocaust, how proud we are and appreciative of the courage that it takes to leave such an important testimony. And certainly, as we grew up, we did have some opportunity, in your discussions with us about some of your experiences-- that certainly left an important mark on our growing up. And everything that you experienced, all the difficulties during that time of the century-- certainly, there's lots to be learned, not only for mankind but certainly for many of us in our family. And there's certainly a lot-- a lot of it from that time and from everything you taught to us about it. There will be so much more for us to teach our children in terms of meanings that that part of history has had for people around the world, and certainly for our family and-- We're just appreciative that you're here and everything that you've brought to bear in the world since that time, and how proud we are and appreciative of the courage that it's taken you all your life to move forward and to deal with the past, and yet to live very much in the here and now with an eye toward the future and with everything that's surrounding you here and everybody that loves you. And we just love you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: We sure do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm going to add something to that. I just wanted to say that I admire my father for his resilience. [CRYING] I'm amazed at the way he found the strength to continue on-- [CRYING] --in his life with such faith. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I know that we can't ever imagine what he went through. But-- [CRYING] --I know that, god forbid-- I'm sure that I could never have handled such a thing. And I know that it must've taken an amazing amount of courage on his part, and strength. And I just wanted to tell him that I love him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any of the children, maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to say something about Zayde?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to say how lucky we are because I know from my peers that most people don't have a relationship with their grandfather that I have with mine. And I really feel like he's my best friend in a lot of ways, and I know I can trust him with everything, and I can tell him everything, and I do. And I think all my brothers and my sisters and my cousins feel that way also, that he's always there for us. And we don't know what we'd do without him. And we hope we'll never know.

words.text: friend in a lot of ways,

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, I would like to thank you very much for joining us. This will be the conclusion of our interview with Emanuel Mittelman. We used eight tapes.

words.text: I would like to thank you
